questions.text: I experience creative frustration as a physical agitation — pacing, restlessness, inability to sit still.
I find it hard to step aside when I believe I am the most capable person to lead.
I find that I do not register the emotional tenor of a handshake or hug.
I shop and pack based on what occurs to me in the moment rather than from a list.
I feel that willpower is like a muscle that grows stronger with regular use.
I feel that the moral pull of suffering is the experience that most clearly reveals the difference between knowing what is right and actually caring about it.
I hold the view that my emotional responses are like a fire that catches fast and burns hot.
I experience life at a comfortable volume — engaged but not flooded.
I find that I do my best thinking when I am focused on my own challenges.
I think of authority as something to be shared, not concentrated.
I have been told that I have a commanding presence.
I rarely notice when someone is speaking louder than necessary to assert dominance.
I share my perspective and then genuinely let the group weigh all viewpoints equally.
I believe that the right response to abundance is to distribute it, not to hoard it.
I tend to share my work in progress early so others can shape it with me.
I precommit to decisions to protect my future self from short-sighted impulses.
I notice that I don't experience the emotional hangovers that others describe after intense days.
I notice that my emotional baseline is remarkably stable from day to day.
I tend to define success in terms of my own benchmarks, not in terms of what I did for others.
I find that my default setting is to consider what others need before what I want.
I believe that hiring help for tasks I dislike frees me to focus on what I do best.
I find that I can evaluate the merits of competing claims for help without being drawn toward the one that is most emotionally vivid.
I can tell when a meeting is happening for show rather than for any real decision-making.
I tend to define what done looks like before anyone else weighs in.
I pick up on when a person glances at someone specific before responding to a question.
I hold the view that composure in the face of human distress is the trait that most directly enables a person to translate good will into good work.
I believe that the most trustworthy assessments of human need come from those who can evaluate without emotional interference.
I feel that even positive experiences can be overwhelming when they arrive with full intensity.
I find that the felt weight of others' suffering is the moral reality that has most powerfully expanded my understanding of what justice requires in practice.
I find that establishing clear procedures prevents mistakes and miscommunication.
I check and respond to messages whenever they arrive, staying in the flow of communication.
I believe that the moral concern that suffering evokes in me is the most honest expression of my values in action.
I would skip my own vacation to cover for a coworker dealing with a family crisis.
I can work effectively in chaotic environments that would distract most people.
I detect when a joke lands differently than the speaker intended.
I tend to assume people are in a neutral mood unless they tell me otherwise.
